revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:仏母院]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Butsumoin 01.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Shingon-shū Daigo-ha|qid=Q10437874|1=Shingon-shū Daigo-ha}}|prefecture={{ill|Tadotsu|qid=Q1357346|1=Tadotsu, Kagawa}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.256244|N|133.733875|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Vairocana|qid=Q239847|1=Vairocana}}}}
'''Butsumoin'''is a temple located in Nishishirakata, Tadotsu Town, Nakatado District, Kagawa Prefecture, Japan. Its mountain name is Hachimanzan, and it is formally known as Hachimanzan Sankakuji Butsumoin. The temple belongs to the Daigo school of Shingon Buddhism, with Dainichi Nyorai as its principal deity. It is an extra pilgrimage site of the Shikoku 88 Temple Pilgrimage and the 17th temple of the New Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage. The temple is said to be the former residence of Tamayori Gozen, the mother of Kobo Daishi (Kukai). '''Principal Deity Mantra:''' "On Abiraunken Bazara Dato Ban" '''Temple Hymn:''' "The sound of heavenly music resonates, Butsumoin, the temple of the Buddha's mother." '''Residence Site Hymn:''' "In the sacred land of Misumi, Tamayori bears the divine child, Daishi." '''Overview:''' Tamayori Gozen is said to have prayed to Hachiman, the tutelary deity of Kumade Hachiman Shrine, where she gave birth to Kukai. The former residence is known as Misumi Residence, now marked by a Buddhist hall. Within the temple grounds, there is "Enazuka" (a mound containing Kukai's umbilical cord) and the well used for his first bath. The main hall houses the "Warabebutsu," a clay Buddha statue said to have been made by Kukai in his childhood. The temple also preserves an imperial plaque inscribed with "Hachiman Daibosatsu" and a sacred rake from Kumade Hachiman Shrine. It is said that Kukai reorganized the temple upon his return from Tang China, naming it Sankakuji and making it the bettoji (associated temple) of Kumade Hachiman Shrine, thus adopting the mountain name Hachimanzan. '''History:''' The temple fell into disrepair during the Sengoku period but was restored by the ascetic Daizenbo. During the Kan'ei era (1638), the temple received the title "Butsumoin" from the Saga Imperial Palace, replacing its previous name, Daizenbo. '''Temple Grounds:''' * Main Gate * Main Hall: Features the principal deity Dainichi Nyorai, with Kobo Daishi on the right and Amida Nyorai on the left. '''Outside the Main Gate:''' * Gomokudo & Memorial Hall: Houses statues of Tamayori Gozen, Kobo Daishi, and Fudo Myo-o. Rebuilt in 2016 due to aging. * Enazuka (Umbilical Cord Mound) * Birth Well * Koshiki Lantern: Features Siddham script representing Kobo Daishi, Amida Nyorai, and Avalokiteshvara. * Warabezuka (Children's Mound): Located about 2 km behind the temple on Hachimanzan, said to be where young Kukai played. '''Adjacent Pilgrimage Sites:''' * '''New Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage:''' ** 16th: Matsuo-ji ** '''17th: Butsumoin''' ** 18th: Zenshoin '''Related Sites:''' * '''Kumade Hachiman Shrine:''' Features a stone monument inscribed with "Birth Shrine of Kobo Daishi" and houses Koma-inu (guardian lion-dogs) designated as cultural properties. * '''Kaiganji:''' Said to be the birthplace of Tamayori Gozen. * '''Zentsuji:''' Another temple associated with Kobo Daishi. '''References:''' * "Shikoku Pilgrimage Solo Journey: Accompanying Two" Map Edition by Miyazaki Tateki, Henro Path Preservation Association, 2010 (9th Edition). * Explanation boards on-site. '''External Links:''' * New Shikoku Mandala Pilgrimage Official Website
{{translated page|ja|仏母院|version=92461134|comment=Imported full ja history}}
